qa_pairs.question: How do I set up an SSH tunnel to a VNC server on a Nibi compute node?
qa_pairs.answer: To set up an SSH tunnel to a VNC server on a Nibi compute node (e.g., c48) from your laptop, use the command: `ssh -J username@nibi.alliancecan.ca -L 5905:localhost:5901 c48`. This forwards local port 5905 to the VNC server's listening port 5901 on the compute node.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if I exit the compute node while my VNC server is running and a tunnel is active?
qa_pairs.answer: If you exit the compute node that your SSH tunnel is connected to, you will no longer be able to connect to the VNC server with `vncviewer`. However, your VNC server will continue to run, so you can regain access by simply starting a new tunnel.

qa_pairs.question: How do I connect to a VNC server from a Linux desktop after setting up an SSH tunnel?
qa_pairs.answer: From a Linux desktop, open a new local terminal window and use the `vncviewer` command to connect, specifying `localhost:port`. For example, if your local forwarded port is 5905, you would run `vncviewer localhost:5905`. You will then be prompted for the VNC authentication password you set earlier.

qa_pairs.question: How do Mac or Windows users connect to a VNC server after setting up an SSH tunnel?
qa_pairs.answer: Mac or Windows users should click the `TigerVNC Viewer` application icon and enter their `localhost:port` information (e.g., `localhost:5905`) in the `VNC viewer: Connection details` popup dialogue box that appears.

qa_pairs.question: What is the default VNC port assumed by the TigerVNC Viewer?
qa_pairs.answer: The `TigerVNC Viewer` assumes a default VNC port of 5900. If you specify 5900 as the local port to be forwarded when starting your SSH tunnel, you can simply enter `localhost` in the viewer's connection details.

qa_pairs.question: Are there any known issues for Windows users regarding VNC port 5900?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, Windows users may find they cannot set up an SSH tunnel on local port 5900 in the first place.

qa_pairs.question: What kind of desktop is presented after connecting with vncviewer, and how do I launch programs?
qa_pairs.answer: Once `vncviewer` connects, you will be presented with a Linux MATE desktop. To launch a terminal, click on the top menu on "Applications -> System Tools -> MATE Terminal." Programs can be launched by invoking their command within a `bash` session, such as `xclock` or `matlab` (after loading its module).

qa_pairs.question: How can I reset my VNC server password?
qa_pairs.answer: To reset your VNC server password, use the `vncpasswd` command from a terminal on the cluster node where the VNC server is running. You will be prompted to enter and verify a new password.

qa_pairs.question: How can I completely remove my VNC configuration and password?
qa_pairs.answer: You can completely remove your VNC configuration, including your password, by deleting your `~/.vnc` directory. The next time you run `vncserver`, you will be prompted to set a new password.

qa_pairs.question: How do I terminate a running VNC server if it's no longer needed?
qa_pairs.answer: To terminate a running VNC server that is no longer needed, use the command `vncserver -kill :DISPLAY#`, where `DISPLAY#` is the display number of the VNC server you wish to kill (e.g., `vncserver -kill :44`). You can find the display number using `vncserver -list`.

qa_pairs.question: How can I kill all VNC servers running under my username on a node instantly?
qa_pairs.answer: To instantly kill all VNC servers running under your username on a specific node, use the command `pkill Xvnc -u $USER`.
